Here is what TORCH_CHECK_* are defined:
```C++

#include <glog/logging.h>

// Additional macros on top of glog
#ifndef NDEBUG
#define TORCH_CHECK_EQ(val1, val2) CHECK_EQ(val1, val2)
#else // !NDEBUG
// These versions generate no code in optimized mode.
#define TORCH_CHECK_EQ(val1, val2) \
  while (false)                    \
  CHECK_EQ(val1, val2)
#endif // NDEBUG
```


This PR broke FB internals. It seems that the real cause of the problem is the change on the semantics/behaviour of `*CHECK*` on Core. See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/82032#issuecomment-1203695164.

Regardless, to resolve the situation I'm issuing a forward fix at [D38383266](https://www.internalfb.com/diff/D38383266).

First of all, very nicely done!
A few things though:
- The simpler/quick creation of Spectrum is a bit dangerous because the units of the arrays are not specified, especially for wavelength, that could be a huge source of errors. I think a mandatory argument "wav_unit" (and maybe "I_unit" should be asked
- There are still some "_noslit" traces in the names of variables, which is irrelevant now (and false when you apply a slit with `inplace=True`)
- The fact now that the slit convoluted version of a calculated spectrum replace the original spectrum by default can be problematic because it is irreversible. I think the default behavior should be `inplace=False`
- Apply_slit() can now be applied multiple time and give nonsense (with inplace=True), this should not be allowed, for exemple with a `slit_convoluted` flag
- Saving a slit convoluted spectrum is often less interesting than the original spectrum, a warning could be print to explain to the user that saving the original spectrum is a better practice.

These last comments will be solved with the apply_slit on the go feature #245

The `# fmt: off/on/skip` might be the most hacky and impossibly-to-maintain complex feature of Black. Writing any sort of robust code here is challenging. This is definitely a step forward, but there's a wart that I'm not sure how to resolve.

My suggestion is to just fix the weird formatting issue as it involves a (IMO) valid use of `# fmt: off/on` and let the other usages break since they don't use it properly`. OTOH, there might be cases with valid usages of `# fmt: off/on` that break by fixing the formatting issue with #2015's example ...

I think the idea of the second part of the change - making the calculation cope with characters with descenders - is fine, but the first part contains too many questionable items: the arbitrary and extra "," and the wrong / arbitrary numbers 3276x. 

Also, I haven't actually checked the calculation - the idea is always that you do one pass to find the extent, a second pass to do the actual drawing. I know `baseline` shouldn't be called that so that's fine, but you removed the `left` variable too... That reminds me - if you want this code to work correctly for arbitrary strings, you need to make one more set of adjustments: besides coping with descenders, you also need to take into account of the left-side-bearing of the first character, and the right-side-bearing of the last character. i.e when the first character draws before its origin, and the last character draws after its advance-width.

I'm a bit confused by all the things happening around `document_type`, `top_level_document_type`, etc. let's discuss it IRL.

There is no documentation on any of these classes. That's fine at this stage, but we will want it before we make an official release (i.e., before we merge `vision` into `master`).

That said, it would be great if we could identify some natural entry point to the transformer toolkit, maybe `allennlp/modules/transformer/__init__.py`, where we can stick some documentation with high-level examples of how to use this. I'm not set on which examples to include, but here are some ideas for things that aren't completely outlandish:
 * Use a 4-layer transformer on top of glove embeddings (with no pre-trained weights).
 * Use the embedding plus the first 4 layers of bert-base-uncased.
 * We have two pieces of text that we want to combine meaningfully. We use the first 8 layers of bert-base-uncased on these separately, then we concatenate the representations and use the final 4 layers on the concatenated sequence. This is a real case, an architecture @HarshTrivedi has experimented with before.

Based on @Frassle's comment I modified this to no longer set defaults during import etc. Now we'll _only_ set Created+Updated during a create, and Updated during an update. With this behavior it's possible and very likely for a resource to have Updated set with a nil Created. This is reasonable if we expect these to represent an exact timestamp and not a best guess.

I took a stab at a lifecycle test that goes through create → refresh → update steps. The timestamps _appear_ to propagate correctly but I feel uncertain. Is there some pathological case to consider that could accidentally set these to nil? Do reads matter? Failed operations? 🤔 

Folks using older CLI releases will of course reset these to nil -- that's unavoidable, and also a problem that lessens over time as people upgrade. We _might_ be able to detect this situation on the SaaS and generate some server-side timestamps, but I haven't looked into it yet.

Edit: one more thought -- we use `time.Now()` in the manifest's timestamp and it's worth confirming we want to keep this consistent and _not_ use UTC for these timestamps. Edit edit: this has been updated to use UTC.

Hey @saswatpp and thanks a lot for the PR! I have some comments inline that make the implementation (hopefully) a little cleaner. On top of that, you need to run our auto-formatters on your code to pass CI. Have a look at our [contributing guide](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md). In short, you can do the following:

```bash
pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install  # activates the auto-formatter for each subsequent commit
pre-commit run --all-files  # only needed once, since you already commited
git commit -am "fix code format"
```

> Also, I couldn't understand what exactly the tests for datasets does just by looking at the code. Do we have some resources for that ?

The test for the datasets are located in `test/test_datasets.py`. From the contributor perspective you need to add a new test case that configures some basics and provides fake data. 

```python
class SUN397TestCase(datasets_utils.ImageDatasetTestCase):
    DATASET_CLASS = datasets.SUN397

    ADDITIONAL_CONFIGS = datasets_utils.combinations_grid(
        split=("train", "test"),
        # There is no need to test all individual partitions, since they all behave the same
        partition=(1, 10, "all"),
    )

    def inject_fake_data(tmp_dir, config):
        ...
```

`inject_fake_data` should prepare `tmp_dir` (in your case the same as `root` in the dataset) in a way that the structure of the files is equal to what would be there if I had instantiated the dataset with `download=True`. It needs to return the number of samples in the dataset. If you want to know more, you can read the [documentation of the underlying test case](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/922db3086e654871c35cd80c2c01eabb65d78475/test/datasets_utils.py#L185).

Here are some cases to test. These first ones should raise an error (ValueError, as usual), but not hang:

```python
"""{"one": 1, "two": 2.2,"""
"""{"one": 1,
"two": 2.2,"""
"""{"one": 1,
"two": 2.2,
"""
"""{"one": 1, "two": 2.2, "three": "THREE"}
{"one": 10, "two": 22,"""
"""{"one": 1, "two": 2.2, "three": "THREE"}
{"one": 10, "two": 22,
"""
"""["one", "two","""
"""["one",
"two","""
"""["one",
"two",
"""
```

And these should recognize that there are two JSON objects in the stream, despite the whitespace:

```python
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}     {"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, \t "two": 2.2}{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}  \t   {"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}\n{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}\n\r{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}     \n     {"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}     \n\r     {"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}{"one": 10, "two": 22}\n'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}{"one": 10, "two": 22}\n\r'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}\n{"one": 10, "two": 22}\n'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}\n\r{"one": 10, "two": 22}\n\r'
'{"one": 1, \n"two": 2.2}{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, \n\r"two": 2.2}{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, \n"two": 2.2}\n{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, \n\r"two": 2.2}\n\r{"one": 10, "two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, \n"two": 2.2}\n{"one": 10, "two": 22}\n'
'{"one": 1, \n\r"two": 2.2}\n\r{"one": 10, "two": 22}\n\r'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}{"one": 10, \n"two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}{"one": 10, \n\r"two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}\n{"one": 10, \n"two": 22}'
'{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}\n\r{"one": 10, \n\r"two": 22}'
'["one", "two"]["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", "two"]\n["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", "two"]\n\r["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", "two"]     ["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", "two"]  \n   ["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", "two"]  \n\r   ["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", \n "two"]["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", \n "two"]\n["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", \n\r "two"]["uno", "dos"]'
'["one", \n\r "two"]\n\r["uno", "dos"]'
'"one""two"'
'"one"\n"two"'
'"one"\n\r"two"'
'"one"     "two"'
'"one"  \n   "two"'
'"one"  \n\r   "two"'
```

If it's too difficult to allow carriage returns within JSON objects and also between them (while not allowing incomplete JSON objects to make the parser hang), we could make the definition of a "JSONS" stream more strict: we could adopt a rule that valid JSON documents must be separated by carriage returns, but allow any whitespace. (That also solves the `\n` vs `\n\r` problem: we can consider `\n` to be the true carriage return and `\r` is just whitespace, like ` ` and `\t`.) That reduces the set of valid examples above. But even with this restrictive rule, blank lines should be allowed and skipped:

```python
"""{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}

{"one": 10, "two": 22}"""
"""{"one": 1, "two": 2.2}

{"one": 10, "two": 22}
"""
"""1
2

3"""
"""1
     2

3
"""
```

That's probably the easiest way to allow the stream to end with a newline.

A couple of concerns:

1) I think the PR description is missing an explanation of the actual problem(s) that the library currently has with uWSGI which would provide a lot more context to the changes (which may render my next concern moot).

2) As far as I can tell, `ddtrace-run` is only broken with uWSGI when threads are spawned in the master process. This occurs only when 1) run-time metrics are enabled since the thread is spawned immediately; or 2) when the AgentWriter thread starts (only happens once a trace is written). Since runtime metrics are not publicly documented (private beta) and it's unlikely that traces are written from the master process(?) it seems like uWSGI + `ddtrace-run` would work in almost all use-cases at the moment (unless I'm missing something).

It seems a bit drastic to me to introduce breaking functionality by making `ddtrace-run` incompatible with uWSGI entirely. It seems like we could do better by checking if runtime metrics are enabled and used under uWSGI and warning/crashing only then.

This looks great! The CSS implementation degrades gracefully on IE11 and otherwise the behaviors are quite similar, so I would support migrating to it entirely rather than maintaining two parallel implementations.

Shorter-term the main question is cost/risk of migration – if either are high, we might want to use both at least until 0.15. Longer-term once things are consolidated, we could even move it to the design system as `KTextTruncator`.

> Multiple lines - a long word with no breaks

The behavior for the CSS implementation looks good here. Flagging that this case has been a source of many bugs over the years, and warrants a careful eye:

* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/5468
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/5464
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/4419


> There's an issue in IE11 with three ellipsis dots not showing in some situations. It seems to be a font issue.

Would you mind filing a separate issue for this? For some context, here is an overview of the intended behavior:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/6903afec0ca3ebdf65fbf0eb96ef5348ed9fc2d2/packages/kolibri-tools/lib/i18n/fonts.py#L589-L603

and the client-side font loading occurs here:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/6903afec0ca3ebdf65fbf0eb96ef5348ed9fc2d2/kolibri/core/assets/src/utils/setupAndLoadFonts.js

> Regarding a11y ... for most techniques, it says that the whole text would be read. 

I've always assumed that the best behavior would be to only read the visible text: if we've made a design decision to truncate it visually, that should probably apply to audible text too? That said, I don't think that either choice is "wrong" and both will result in a reasonable experience for all users.

If we wanted to get really fancy here, we could use `aria-hidden="true"` and make a separate off-screen copy of the text for screen readers and truncate that based on some metric like `maxLen = maxLines * 50`.

Just skimmed the direct task transport class so far, but I can look more closely as you go through the current comments. My major comment is about keeping the control flow in the CoreWorkerDirectTaskSubmitter clear. One way to think about it is to make sure that we have certain invariants, and that the code to uphold those invariants is factored out into its own method. Like:

if there are tasks in the queue and non-full workers -> dispatch to a worker
if there are tasks in the queue and the workers are full -> request a new worker (same as before)
if there are no tasks in the queue stealable tasks, but no thieves -> request a new worker
if there are no tasks in the queue, stealable tasks, and a thief -> steal
else: return the worker

I think the code does match these invariants, but it's a bit hard to tell because it's hard to reason about where the parts on the right side are getting called.

@StrikerRUS whenever you have time, could you give your opinion? I can give you the relevant context so you don't have to re-read all the discussion here and in #3768. 

In #3766, I added a function `_find_open_port()` that the Dask package uses to build the `machines` list. But as I noted in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/3766#discussion_r557736104, the approach adds some overhead. It currently runs that check sequentially. So for a 3-worker cluster, it will go "find port on `w1` then find port on `w2` then find port on `w3`". This means there is O(n_workers) overhead introduced.

This PR proposes running a function to find a random port on every worker simultaneously, so in theory the overhead of setting up `machines` becomes O(1). This is faster, but it also means the ports used for `machines` are totally unrelated to `local_listen_port`.

In https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/3823#issuecomment-768816075 I told @jmoralez that in my opinion, this faster behavior should be the default, but that we need to still give people who are limited by firewall rules the ability to specify a `local_listen_port` and know that only that port and those near it will be searched.

So we settled on the current state of this PR, which is:

1. if local_listen_port is missing from parameters, use the faster approach
1. if local_listen_port is set to 12400 (LightGBM's default), use the faster approach
1. if local_listen_port is set to a value other than 12400, use the `_find_open_port()` approach where we specifically search 1000 ports from `local_listen_port` to `local_listen_port + 999`.

Added some comments where I saw that the code style or general usage diverges too far from the current standard or where we already have specific patterns in place.

One issue I've noticed is that the resource cleanup needs to be adjusted to be more robust. A typical example for a resource leak here would be:
```
# 1. create secret
# 2. do something with secret
# 3. cleanup (delete secret)
```

If anything in step 2 fails, the cleanup will be skipped and resources start leaking. This affects LocalStack as well but more importantly your AWS account might end up cluttered with these.

There are a few patterns for this in place right now:

1. Create a fixture
2. Using the `cleanups` fixture and appending cleanup callables (in the simplest case just a lambda performing the same cleanup you'd otherwise do)
3. try/finally blocks (these additionally need to account for the fact that when cleaning up multiple resources, subsequent resource cleanup has to stay unaffected)

Option 1/2 are in general preferred over 3
